ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

Downstream writers will use the query_postgres tool to inspect source data while implementing your plan.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 1, 803612, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 1, 803620, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 1, 803743, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f394b-924a-7658-a67f-7428808f9ff7', conversation_id='019f394b-924a-7658-a67f-7427eea4a741')

data.overview: This plan outlines the mapping from the `fixture_master_v2_src` database to the target Salesforce-style schema. A key aspect is the consistent generation of Salesforce `Id`s from source natural keys (e.g., customer numbers, contact IDs) using a deterministic `GENERATE_SF_ID` function. This function must produce the same ID for the same input natural key across all tables to ensure foreign key integrity. For enum-typed target columns, source values will be mapped via `CASE` statements, with `NULL` as the fallback for unmatchable values unless the target column is `NOT NULL`, in which case a sensible default is provided. Dates will be parsed flexibly and formatted to `YYYY-MM-DD`, preferring `NULL` for unparseable dates. Amounts will be cleaned of currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Standard Salesforce fields (`CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted`) will default to `NULL` or `0` respectively, as no source data is available for them. All `Legacy_*__c` fields will directly capture the source natural key for data verification purposes.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 29, 763687, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- target_table: name of the model

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

Overview: This plan outlines the mapping from the `fixture_master_v2_src` database to the target Salesforce-style schema. A key aspect is the consistent generation of Salesforce `Id`s from source natural keys (e.g., customer numbers, contact IDs) using a deterministic `GENERATE_SF_ID` function. Standard Salesforce fields (`CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted`) will default to `NULL` or `0` respectively, as no source data is available for them. ### एनीमिया क्या है?

एनीमिया एक ऐसी स्थिति है जिसमें आपके रक्त में स्वस्थ लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं की कमी होती है। ये कोशिकाएं शरीर के अंगों और ऊतकों तक ऑक्सीजन ले जाती हैं। एनीमिया से थकान, कमजोरी, सांस लेने में तकलीफ और चक्कर आ सकते हैं।

### एनीमिया के कारण:

* **आयरन की कमी:** यह एनीमिया का सबसे आम कारण है। शरीर को पर्याप्त आयरन नहीं मिलता, जो हीमोग्लोबिन बनाने के लिए आवश्यक है।
* **विटामिन की कमी:** विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट की कमी भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती है।
* **रक्त की हानि:** अत्यधिक रक्तस्राव, जैसे मासिक धर्म, चोट या आंतरिक रक्तस्राव, एनीमिया का कारण बन सकता है।
* **पुरानी बीमारियाँ:** किडनी रोग, कैंसर या ऑटोइम्यून बीमारियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।
* **आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ:** सिकल सेल एनीमिया और थैलेसीमिया जैसी कुछ आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।

### एनीमिया के लिए सुझाव:

एनीमिया के इलाज के लिए, डॉक्टर की सलाह लेना बहुत महत्वपूर्ण है। डॉक्टर आपके एनीमिया के कारण का निदान करेंगे और उचित उपचार योजना सुझाएँगे। यहाँ कुछ सामान्य सुझाव दिए गए हैं जो एनीमिया के प्रबंधन में सहायक हो सकते हैं:

**1. आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ:** पालक, मेथी, सरसों का साग।
* **दालें और फलियाँ:** मसूर दाल, चना, राजमा, सोयाबीन।
* **मांस:** लाल मांस (बीफ, भेड़ का मांस), चिकन, मछली।
* **मेवे और बीज:** कद्दू के बीज, तिल, बादाम।
* **सूखे मेवे:** किशमिश, खजूर, खुबानी।
* **अंडे:** आयरन का अच्छा स्रोत हैं।
* **फोर्टिफाइड अनाज:** कुछ अनाज आयरन से समृद्ध होते हैं।

**2. विटामिन सी युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

विटामिन सी शरीर को आयरन को बेहतर ढंग से अवशोषित करने में मदद करता है। आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थों के साथ विटामिन सी वाले खाद्य पदार्थ खाएँ।

* **खट्टे फल:** संतरा, नींबू, चकोतरा।
* **जामुन:** स्ट्रॉबेरी, ब्लूबेरी।
* **मिर्च:** शिमला मिर्च।
* **ब्रोकोली और टमाटर।**

**3. विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **विटामिन बी12:** मांस, मछली, अंडे, डेयरी उत्पाद और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज। शाकाहारियों और वेगन्स को सप्लीमेंट्स की आवश्यकता हो सकती है।
* **फोलेट:** पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ, ब्रोकोली, दालें, फलियाँ, एवोकाडो और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज।

**4. आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स:**

यदि आपके डॉक्टर सलाह देते हैं, तो आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स लें। इन्हें खाली पेट या विटामिन सी के साथ लेने से अवशोषण बेहतर होता है। डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना सप्लीमेंट्स न लें, क्योंकि अतिरिक्त आयरन हानिकारक हो सकता है।

**5. आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित करने वाले खाद्य पदार्थों से बचें:**

* **चाय और कॉफी:** इनमें टैनिन होते हैं जो आयरन के अवशोषण को कम कर सकते हैं। इन्हें भोजन के समय से दूर पिएँ।
* **दूध और डेयरी उत्पाद:** इनमें कैल्शियम होता है जो आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित कर सकता है।

**6. नियमित व्यायाम:**

नियमित व्यायाम रक्त परिसंचरण में सुधार करता है और लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं के उत्पादन को बढ़ावा देता है।

**7. पर्याप्त नींद:**

अच्छी नींद शरीर को ठीक होने और ऊर्जा स्तर को बनाए रखने में मदद करती है।

**8. धूम्रपान और शराब से बचें:**

ये दोनों ही एनीमिया को बदतर बना सकते हैं।

**9. नियमित स्वास्थ्य जाँच:**

नियमित रूप से डॉक्टर से मिलें और अपने रक्त परीक्षण करवाएँ ताकि एनीमिया के स्तर की निगरानी की जा सके और उपचार की प्रभावशीलता का आकलन किया जा सके।

**महत्वपूर्ण नोट:** यह जानकारी केवल सामान्य मार्गदर्शन के लिए है। एनीमिया के सटीक निदान और उपचार के लिए हमेशा योग्य स्वास्थ्य पेशेवर से परामर्श करें।

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 29, 763834, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

### एनीमिया क्या है?

एनीमिया एक ऐसी स्थिति है जिसमें आपके रक्त में स्वस्थ लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं की कमी होती है। ये कोशिकाएं शरीर के अंगों और ऊतकों तक ऑक्सीजन ले जाती हैं। एनीमिया से थकान, कमजोरी, सांस लेने में तकलीफ और चक्कर आ सकते हैं।

### एनीमिया के कारण:

* **आयरन की कमी:** यह एनीमिया का सबसे आम कारण है। शरीर को पर्याप्त आयरन नहीं मिलता, जो हीमोग्लोबिन बनाने के लिए आवश्यक है।
* **विटामिन की कमी:** विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट की कमी भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती है।
* **रक्त की हानि:** अत्यधिक रक्तस्राव, जैसे मासिक धर्म, चोट या आंतरिक रक्तस्राव, एनीमिया का कारण बन सकता है।
* **पुरानी बीमारियाँ:** किडनी रोग, कैंसर या ऑटोइम्यून बीमारियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।
* **आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ:** सिकल सेल एनीमिया और थैलेसीमिया जैसी कुछ आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।

### एनीमिया के लिए सुझाव:

एनीमिया के इलाज के लिए, डॉक्टर की सलाह लेना बहुत महत्वपूर्ण है। डॉक्टर आपके एनीमिया के कारण का निदान करेंगे और उचित उपचार योजना सुझाएँगे। यहाँ कुछ सामान्य सुझाव दिए गए हैं जो एनीमिया के प्रबंधन में सहायक हो सकते हैं:

**1. आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ:** पालक, मेथी, सरसों का साग।
* **दालें और फलियाँ:** मसूर दाल, चना, राजमा, सोयाबीन।
* **मांस:** लाल मांस (बीफ, भेड़ का मांस), चिकन, मछली।
* **मेवे और बीज:** कद्दू के बीज, तिल, बादाम।
* **सूखे मेवे:** किशमिश, खजूर, खुबानी।
* **अंडे:** आयरन का अच्छा स्रोत हैं।
* **फोर्टिफाइड अनाज:** कुछ अनाज आयरन से समृद्ध होते हैं।

**2. विटामिन सी युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

विटामिन सी शरीर को आयरन को बेहतर ढंग से अवशोषित करने में मदद करता है। आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थों के साथ विटामिन सी वाले खाद्य पदार्थ खाएँ।

* **खट्टे फल:** संतरा, नींबू, चकोतरा।
* **जामुन:** स्ट्रॉबेरी, ब्लूबेरी।
* **मिर्च:** शिमला मिर्च।
* **ब्रोकोली और टमाटर।**

**3. विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **विटामिन बी12:** मांस, मछली, अंडे, डेयरी उत्पाद और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज। शाकाहारियों और वेगन्स को सप्लीमेंट्स की आवश्यकता हो सकती है।
* **फोलेट:** पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ, ब्रोकोली, दालें, फलियाँ, एवोकाडो और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज।

**4. आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स:**

यदि आपके डॉक्टर सलाह देते हैं, तो आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स लें। इन्हें खाली पेट या विटामिन सी के साथ लेने से अवशोषण बेहतर होता है। डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना सप्लीमेंट्स न लें, क्योंकि अतिरिक्त आयरन हानिकारक हो सकता है।

**5. आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित करने वाले खाद्य पदार्थों से बचें:**

* **चाय और कॉफी:** इनमें टैनिन होते हैं जो आयरन के अवशोषण को कम कर सकते हैं। इन्हें भोजन के समय से दूर पिएँ।
* **दूध और डेयरी उत्पाद:** इनमें कैल्शियम होता है जो आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित कर सकता है।

**6. नियमित व्यायाम:**

नियमित व्यायाम रक्त परिसंचरण में सुधार करता है और लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं के उत्पादन को बढ़ावा देता है।

**7. पर्याप्त नींद:**

अच्छी नींद शरीर को ठीक होने और ऊर्जा स्तर को बनाए रखने में मदद करती है।

**8. धूम्रपान और शराब से बचें:**

ये दोनों ही एनीमिया को बदतर बना सकते हैं।

**9. नियमित स्वास्थ्य जाँच:**

नियमित रूप से डॉक्टर से मिलें और अपने रक्त परीक्षण करवाएँ ताकि एनीमिया के स्तर की निगरानी की जा सके और उपचार की प्रभावशीलता का आकलन किया जा सके।

**महत्वपूर्ण नोट:** यह जानकारी केवल सामान्य मार्गदर्शन के लिए है। एनीमिया के सटीक निदान और उपचार के लिए हमेशा योग्य स्वास्थ्य पेशेवर से परामर्श करें।

### एनीमिया के कारण:

* **आयरन की कमी:** यह एनीमिया का सबसे आम कारण है। शरीर को पर्याप्त आयरन नहीं मिलता, जो हीमोग्लोबिन बनाने के लिए आवश्यक है।

* **पुरानी बीमारियाँ:** किडनी रोग, कैंसर या ऑटोइम्यून बीमारियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।
* **आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ:** सिकल सेल एनीमिया और थैलेसीमिया जैसी कुछ आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।

* **दालें और फलियाँ:** मसूर दाल, चना, राजमा, सोयाबीन।

**2. विटामिन सी युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 51, 718858, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql
normalize.sql
```

dbt error:
Database Error in model Opportunity (models/Opportunity.sql)
  syntax error at or near "normalize"
  LINE 12:     normalize.sql
               ^
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Opportunity.sql

When fixing errors:

- Ensure every mixed-case output column uses a double-quoted alias: `expr AS "ColumnName"`.

- Ensure every CAST includes `AS <type>`: `CAST(expr AS DOUBLE PRECISION)`, not `CAST(expr)`.

- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key — evaluators join on these.

- `AccountId` / `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.

- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 51, 718908, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Project__c` failed.

```sql
 एनीमिया के लिए कुछ सुझाव दें।

### एनीमिया क्या है?

एनीमिया एक ऐसी स्थिति है जिसमें आपके रक्त में स्वस्थ लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं की कमी होती है। ये कोशिकाएं शरीर के अंगों और ऊतकों तक ऑक्सीजन ले जाती हैं। एनीमिया से थकान, कमजोरी, सांस लेने में तकलीफ और चक्कर आ सकते हैं।

### एनीमिया के कारण:

* **आयरन की कमी:** यह एनीमिया का सबसे आम कारण है। शरीर को पर्याप्त आयरन नहीं मिलता, जो हीमोग्लोबिन बनाने के लिए आवश्यक है।
* **विटामिन की कमी:** विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट की कमी भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती है।
* **रक्त की हानि:** अत्यधिक रक्तस्राव, जैसे मासिक धर्म, चोट या आंतरिक रक्तस्राव, एनीमिया का कारण बन सकता है।
* **पुरानी बीमारियाँ:** किडनी रोग, कैंसर या ऑटोइम्यून बीमारियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।
* **आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ:** सिकल सेल एनीमिया और थैलेसीमिया जैसी कुछ आनुवंशिक स्थितियाँ भी एनीमिया का कारण बन सकती हैं।

### एनीमिया के लिए सुझाव:

एनीमिया के इलाज के लिए, डॉक्टर की सलाह लेना बहुत महत्वपूर्ण है। डॉक्टर आपके एनीमिया के कारण का निदान करेंगे और उचित उपचार योजना सुझाएँगे। यहाँ कुछ सामान्य सुझाव दिए गए हैं जो एनीमिया के प्रबंधन में सहायक हो सकते हैं:

**1. आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ:** पालक, मेथी, सरसों का साग।
* **दालें और फलियाँ:** मसूर दाल, चना, राजमा, सोयाबीन।
* **मांस:** लाल मांस (बीफ, भेड़ का मांस), चिकन, मछली।
* **मेवे और बीज:** कद्दू के बीज, तिल, बादाम।
* **सूखे मेवे:** किशमिश, खजूर, खुबानी।
* **अंडे:** आयरन का अच्छा स्रोत हैं।
* **फोर्टिफाइड अनाज:** कुछ अनाज आयरन से समृद्ध होते हैं।

**2. विटामिन सी युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

विटामिन सी शरीर को आयरन को बेहतर ढंग से अवशोषित करने में मदद करता है। आयरन युक्त खाद्य पदार्थों के साथ विटामिन सी वाले खाद्य पदार्थ खाएँ।

* **खट्टे फल:** संतरा, नींबू, चकोतरा।
* **जामुन:** स्ट्रॉबेरी, ब्लूबेरी।
* **मिर्च:** शिमला मिर्च।
* **ब्रोकोली और टमाटर।**

**3. विटामिन बी12 और फोलेट युक्त खाद्य पदार्थ:**

* **विटामिन बी12:** मांस, मछली, अंडे, डेयरी उत्पाद और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज। शाकाहारियों और वेगन्स को सप्लीमेंट्स की आवश्यकता हो सकती है।
* **फोलेट:** पत्तेदार सब्जियाँ, ब्रोकोली, दालें, फलियाँ, एवोकाडो और फोर्टिफाइड अनाज।

**4. आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स:**

यदि आपके डॉक्टर सलाह देते हैं, तो आयरन सप्लीमेंट्स लें। इन्हें खाली पेट या विटामिन सी के साथ लेने से अवशोषण बेहतर होता है। डॉक्टर की सलाह के बिना सप्लीमेंट्स न लें, क्योंकि अतिरिक्त आयरन हानिकारक हो सकता है।

**5. आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित करने वाले खाद्य पदार्थों से बचें:**

* **चाय और कॉफी:** इनमें टैनिन होते हैं जो आयरन के अवशोषण को कम कर सकते हैं। इन्हें भोजन के समय से दूर पिएँ।
* **दूध और डेयरी उत्पाद:** इनमें कैल्शियम होता है जो आयरन के अवशोषण को बाधित कर सकता है।

**6. नियमित व्यायाम:**

नियमित व्यायाम रक्त परिसंचरण में सुधार करता है और लाल रक्त कोशिकाओं के उत्पादन को बढ़ावा देता है।

**7. पर्याप्त नींद:**

अच्छी नींद शरीर को ठीक होने और ऊर्जा स्तर को बनाए रखने में मदद करती है।

**8. धूम्रपान और शराब से बचें:**

ये दोनों ही एनीमिया को बदतर बना सकते हैं।

**9. नियमित स्वास्थ्य जाँच:**

नियमित रूप से डॉक्टर से मिलें और अपने रक्त परीक्षण करवाएँ ताकि एनीमिया के स्तर की निगरानी की जा सके और उपचार की प्रभावशीलता का आकलन किया जा सके।

**महत्वपूर्ण नोट:** यह जानकारी केवल सामान्य मार्गदर्शन के लिए है। एनीमिया के सटीक निदान और उपचार के लिए हमेशा योग्य स्वास्थ्य पेशेवर से परामर्श करें।


```

SELECT
    MD5(p.projekt_kennung) AS "Id",
    p.projektname AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('active', 'aktiv') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('abgeschlossen', 'completed') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('planung', 'in planung', 'in planning') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('on hold', 'pausiert') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('cancelled', 'storniert') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(CAST(p.go_live_datum AS DATE), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    MD5(p.kunden_kennung) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(p.opp_kennung_ref) AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.projekt_kennung AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} AS p
WHERE
    p.projekt_kennung IS NOT NULL
AND
    p.projektname IS NOT NULL;
', 'target_table': 'Project__c'}, tool_call_id='pyd_ai_7240a4e734264142ba936b6b414c4e0c', provider_name='google-cloud', provider_details={'thought_signature': ''})], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=5469, cache_read_tokens=4459, output_tokens=2368, details={'cached_content_tokens': 4459, 'thoughts_tokens': 1400, 'text_prompt_tokens': 5469, 'text_cache_tokens': 4459, 'text_candidates_tokens': 968}), model_name='gemini-2.5-flash', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 19, 10, 481514, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='google-cloud', provider_url='https://aiplatform.googleapis.com/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'STOP', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 18, 58, 591690, tzinfo=TzInfo(0)), 'traffic_type': 'ON_DEMAND'}, provider_response_id='whtMasqOJJrqkdUPk6Lt2Qk', finish_reason='stop')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 19, 17, 417327, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 19, 17, 417362, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Project__c` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(p.projekt_kennung) AS "Id",
    p.projektname AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('active', 'aktiv') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('abgeschlossen', 'completed') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('planung', 'in planung', 'in planning') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('on hold', 'pausiert') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(p.projektstatus) IN ('cancelled', 'storniert') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(CAST(p.go_live_datum AS DATE), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    MD5(p.kunden_kennung) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(p.opp_kennung_ref) AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.projekt_kennung AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} AS p
WHERE
    p.projekt_kennung IS NOT NULL
AND
    p.projektname IS NOT NULL;

```

source currency representations, defaulting to 'EUR' if unmapped or null.", 'source_columns': ['waehrungscode'], 'target_column': 'CurrencyIsoCode'}, {'source_columns': ['kunden_ref'], 'target_column': 'AccountId', 'explanation': 'The Salesforce-style Account ID associated with the opportunity, derived by MD5 hashing the customer reference ID from the source.'}, {'explanation': 'The original opportunity identifier from the source system.', 'target_column': 'Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c', 'source_columns': ['opp_kennung']}, {'explanation': 'Placeholder as there is no corresponding source column.', 'target_column': 'CreatedDate', 'source_columns': []}, {'explanation': 'Placeholder as there is no corresponding source column.', 'target_column': 'LastModifiedDate', 'source_columns': []}, {'explanation': 'Hardcoded to 0 as there is no corresponding source column.', 'target_column': 'IsDeleted', 'source_columns': []}], 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    o.titel AS "Name",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen', 'closed-won') THEN 'Closed Won'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'closed-lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
            ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
        END,
        'Prospecting'
    ) AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN o.zieldatum
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.auftragswert IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                CASE
                    WHEN o.auftragswert ~ '^[^\]*\,[^\]]*$' THEN -- Contains comma, but no dot (e.g. 123,45)
                        REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert), ',', '.')
                    WHEN o.auftragswert ~ '^[^\]*\.[^\]*\,[^\]]*$' THEN -- Contains dot then comma (European: 1.234,56)
                        REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                    ELSE -- Contains dot, no comma, or neither (American: 1234.56 or 1234)
                        TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert)
                END
            AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
            ELSE 'EUR' -- Default for NOT NULL CurrencyIsoCode
        END,
        'EUR'
    ) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(o.kunden_ref) AS "AccountId",
    o.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS o
', 'target_table': 'Opportunity', 'reasoning': 'The model transforms the `master_opportunities` source table into the `Opportunity` target schema. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    o.titel AS "Name",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen', 'closed-won') THEN 'Closed Won'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'closed-lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
            ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
        END,
        'Prospecting'
    ) AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN o.zieldatum
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.auftragswert IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                CASE
                    WHEN o.auftragswert ~ '^[^\]*\,[^\]]*$' THEN -- Contains comma, but no dot (e.g. 123,45)
                        REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert), ',', '.')
                    WHEN o.auftragswert ~ '^[^\]*\.[^\]*\,[^\]]*$' THEN -- Contains dot then comma (European: 1.234,56)
                        REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                    ELSE -- Contains dot, no comma, or neither (American: 1234.56 or 1234)
                        TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM o.auftragswert)
                END
            AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
            ELSE 'EUR' -- Default for NOT NULL CurrencyIsoCode
        END,
        'EUR'
    ) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(o.kunden_ref) AS "AccountId",
    o.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS o

```

Previous SQL:
```sql
normalize_whitespace='''
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    o.titel AS "Name",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen', 'closed-won') THEN 'Closed Won'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'closed-lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
            ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
        END,
        'Prospecting'
    ) AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN o.zieldatum
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.auftragswert IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (
                SELECT
                    CASE
                        -- Check if the final cleaned string is a valid number before casting
                        WHEN cleaned_num_str ~ '^[+\-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)$' THEN CAST(cleaned_num_str AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                        ELSE NULL
                    END
                FROM (
                    SELECT
                        CASE
                            -- European format: 1.234,56 -> 1234.56
                            WHEN normalized_val ~ '^\d+\.\d+,\d+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(normalized_val, '.'), ',', '.')
                            -- European format: 123,45 -> 123.45
                            WHEN normalized_val ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN REPLACE(normalized_val, ',', '.')
                            -- Default to American/plain (1234.56 or 1234)
                            ELSE normalized_val
                        END AS cleaned_num_str
                    FROM (
                        -- Remove common currency prefixes/suffixes and extra spaces
                        SELECT
                            TRIM(
                                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                                    LOWER(o.auftragswert),
                                    '^(eur|gbp|usd|chf|€|£|$)\s*|\s*(eur|gbp|usd|chf|€|£|$)$',
                                    '',
                                    'g'
                                )
                            ) AS normalized_val
                    ) AS _s1
                ) AS _s2
            )
    END AS "Amount",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
            ELSE 'EUR' -- Default for NOT NULL CurrencyIsoCode
        END,
        'EUR'
    ) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(o.kunden_ref) AS "AccountId",
    o.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS o
'''
```

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    o.titel AS "Name",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen', 'closed-won') THEN 'Closed Won'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'closed-lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN LOWER(o.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
            ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
        END,
        'Prospecting'
    ) AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN o.zieldatum
            WHEN o.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(o.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN o.auftragswert IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (
                SELECT
                    CASE
                        -- Check if the final cleaned string is a valid number before casting
                        WHEN cleaned_num_str ~ '^[+\-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)$' THEN CAST(cleaned_num_str AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                        ELSE NULL
                    END
                FROM (
                    SELECT
                        CASE
                            -- European format: 1.234,56 -> 1234.56
                            WHEN normalized_val ~ '^\d+\.\d+,\d+$' THEN REPLACE(REPLACE(normalized_val, '.'), ',', '.')
                            -- European format: 123,45 -> 123.45
                            WHEN normalized_val ~ '^\d+,\d+$' THEN REPLACE(normalized_val, ',', '.')
                            -- Default to American/plain (1234.56 or 1234)
                            ELSE normalized_val
                        END AS cleaned_num_str
                    FROM (
                        -- Remove common currency prefixes/suffixes and extra spaces
                        SELECT
                            TRIM(
                                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                                    LOWER(o.auftragswert),
                                    '^(eur|gbp|usd|chf|€|£|$)\s*|\s*(eur|gbp|usd|chf|€|£|$)$',
                                    '',
                                    'g'
                                )
                            ) AS normalized_val
                    ) AS _s1
                ) AS _s2
            )

            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
            WHEN LOWER(o.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
            ELSE 'EUR' -- Default for NOT NULL CurrencyIsoCode
        END,
        'EUR'
    ) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(o.kunden_ref) AS "AccountId",
    o.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS o

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).

- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.

- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'ABGESCHLOSSEN' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'PAUSIERT') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'CANCELLED' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_datum
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    MD5(p.kunden_kennung) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(p.opp_kennung_ref) AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.projekt_kennung AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    TO_CHAR(NOW(), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS.MS"Z"') AS "CreatedDate",
    TO_CHAR(NOW(), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS.MS"Z"') AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
